Fern Polrrrcel PRACrrcEs ConaurssroN
428 J Street . Suite 620 r Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 r Fax (916) 322-0886
Ivne 14,2007
Diane R. Gladwell, CMC
President
Gladwell Govemment Services, Inc.
P.O. Box 62
Lake Arrowhead, CA 92352-0062
RE: Your Request for Advice
Our FiIe No. G.07-101
Dear Ms. Gladwell:
, Thank you for your letter dated June 8, 2007 concerning the Political Reform
Act's' (the "Act") conflict-of-interest provisions.
You ask the Commission to reconsider its long-standing advice that there is no
violation of these provisions when a public official participateJin a gorr".nment decision
on employee union compensation and the official's spouse is a public employee and
union member who will be affected by the govemment decision.
Your request appears to be partly based on a recent opinion issued by the
Attorney General concluding that a board member of a public agency, who is also a
retired employee of the agency, is financially interested in his boardis decision affecting
the official's existing pension plan. It appears the Attorney General's analysis was done
under Govemment Code Section 1090, which is a conflict-of-interest law outside the Act
and not subject to the Commission's jurisdiction.
As you ate aware, the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions generally prohibit a
public official from participating in a government decision in which he or she has a
financial interest (Section 87100). Section 87103(c) provides that asource of income to
an official of $500 or more in the pior 12 months is a financial interest for purposes of
the conflict-of-interest rules and this includes the official's community property share in
his or her spouse's income. Thus, as a general rule, for purposes of the Act,s conflict-of-
interest rules an official has a financial interest in sources olir,.om" to his or her spouse.
I Govemment Code Sections 81000 through gl}14. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, Sections
18109 through18997 of the California Code of Regulations.
g9


File No. c-07-101
Page No. 2
However, Section 52030(b)(2) exempts from the Act's definition of ..income,,
"[s]alary. . . or other similar benefit payments received from a state, local, or federal
government agency. . .." Consequently, with certain exceptions, the Commission does
not apply the Act's conflict-of-interest prohibition when a publicofficial participates in a
govefllment decision affecting salary or other employment benefits provided by a
government employer to the official or the official's spouse. However, the Commission
does apply this rule in some situations, such as decisions to hire, fire, promote, demote, or
take disciplinary action against the official or spouse, or in some cases involving setting a
salary different from other persons in the ra*e "lu.rification 
(see Regulation 1g705.5O)).
Your request is timely in that the Commission's legal staff plans in the near future
to informally review the rules on this issue to determine wlether our regulations can and
should be modified.-If we think changes are necessary and permitted uy taw we will
present thern to the Commission for consideration at u fut r" Commission meeting.
Nevertheless, because of the statutory exception for government salary and benefits
discussed above, we lack the authority to make *y *1" that will deviate from the
statute's requirements. Only a legislative amendment or vote by the electorate through an
initiative can alter this exception.
Should you have additional questions or require further information, please
contad me at your convenience.
Hallabrin
General Counsel
Legal Division
SHjgl

sections.question: Whether the Commission should reconsider its long-standing advice that there is no violation of the Political Reform Act's conflict-of-interest provisions when a public official participates in a government decision on employee union compensation and the official's spouse is a public employee and union member who will be affected by the government decision.
sections.conclusion: The Commission does not apply the Act's conflict-of-interest prohibition when a public official participates in a government decision affecting salary or other employment benefits provided by a government employer to the official or the spouse, due to the statutory exemption in Section 52030(b)(2) for government salary and benefits. However, this rule does not apply in certain situations such as decisions to hire, fire, promote, demote, or take disciplinary action against the official or spouse, or decisions setting a salary different from other persons in the same classification. The Commission lacks the authority to deviate from the statute's requirements; only a legislative amendment or voter initiative can alter this exception.
sections.question_synthetic: Should the FPPC reconsider its position that a public official has no conflict-of-interest violation when participating in government decisions affecting their spouse's employment compensation as a public employee and union member?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Under the Political Reform Act's statutory exemption for government salary and benefits (Section 52030(b)(2)), the FPPC cannot apply its conflict-of-interest prohibition to most decisions affecting an official's spouse's government employment compensation, though exceptions exist for hiring, firing, promotion, demotion, disciplinary actions, and salary-setting decisions that differ from comparable positions. The Commission lacks authority to modify this rule without legislative or electoral action.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains clear QUESTION and CONCLUSION sections. Letter is from FPPC General Counsel responding to requestor's inquiry. Minor OCR issues noted (e.g., 'gorr".nment' should be 'government', 'ra*e "lu.rification' should be 'rate classification') but meaning is clear. File number format is G.07-101 with page numbering indicating multi-page document.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether the Commission should reconsider its long-standing advice that there is no violation of the Political Reform Act's conflict-of-interest provisions when a public official participates in a government decision on employee union compensation and the official's spouse is a public employee and union member who will be affected by the government decision.

CONCLUSION: The Commission does not apply the Act's conflict-of-interest prohibition when a public official participates in a government decision affecting salary or other employment benefits provided by a government employer to the official or the spouse, due to the statutory exemption in Section 52030(b)(2) for government salary and benefits. However, this rule does not apply in certain situations such as decisions to hire, fire, promote, demote, or take disciplinary action against the official or spouse, or decisions setting a salary different from other persons in the same classification. The Commission lacks the authority to deviate from the statute's requirements; only a legislative amendment or voter initiative can alter this exception.

embedding.summary: The FPPC declines to reconsider its position on conflict-of-interest provisions when a public official participates in decisions affecting their spouse's government employment compensation, citing a statutory exemption for government salary and benefits that limits the Commission's authority to modify the rule.
